role_summary: Schoolteacher at Marshall High School in Tchula, Mississippi (Holmes County), after a student-teaching practicum in Belzoni (Humphreys County); mother of one of the children who integrated the Tchula public school in 1965 (the school was burned twice in response); later elected to the Holmes County school board.
ai_reading: Burns's passages are most often retrieved alongside Rosie Head (entry 111) and Euvester Simpson (entry 41), which tie as her top neighbors by appearance count across her transcript's chunks, with Robert G. Clark Jr. (entry 107) next; her single highest-similarity passage match is to Walter Bruce (entry 123) at cosine 0.819. Three of those four (Head, Bruce, Clark) are Holmes County, Mississippi figures, and the Head pairing is the most concrete: Rosie Head managed the Mileston Community Center and worked in the Child Development Group of Mississippi (CDGM) Head Start program, the same Mileston meeting hall and the same CDGM that Burns names in her own testimony, so the embedding is tracking a shared local institution rather than a shared movement label. The axis readings carry the genuine surprise. On the Sacred Framing vs Secular Framing axis the embedding places Burns toward the secular and political-framing pole (position +0.0595, 111th of 136 toward that pole) even though her testimony is dense with church material (a Baptist church, a Methodist church, and a sanctified church every Sunday, and a closing "God has been good to me"); the vector signature appears to be driven by her school-board, curriculum, and economic-development content rather than her churchgoing. On the Southern Struggle vs Northern Struggle axis she reads toward the Northern pole (position +0.08, 107th of 136), an unexpected placement for a rural Holmes County figure that likely reflects her extended Chicago residence and the cross-county and out-of-state migration research she describes. The page's other distinctive thread is the teacher-as-organizer-under-job-threat material: Burns describes being warned she would lose her teaching job if she did not leave the Freedom workers alone, a documentation of the economic-retaliation pressure on Black professionals that is distinct from the front-line-organizer mode of most of her neighbors.
biographical_paragraph: Burns was born in Greenwood, Mississippi, and by about age three her family moved to Humphreys County, where her father acquired roughly 123 acres through the federal Farm Security Administration land-purchase program of the early 1940s [src: 1]. She finished high school in Louise, Mississippi, took her degree at Mississippi Valley State College, and did her student-teaching practicum in Belzoni, the Humphreys County seat where she had grown up [src: 1]. After a brief stay in Chicago she returned to Mississippi and was hired by principal S.B. Marshall to teach at Marshall High School in Tchula, in Holmes County, where she settled [src: 1]. Her oral history records that her direct movement involvement was constrained by the risk to that teaching job: her husband worked openly with SNCC and the Child Development Group of Mississippi (CDGM), Holmes County organizers met at her house after dark, and a Greenwood organizer she identifies as John Ball warned her she would lose her job if she did not leave the Freedom workers alone [src: 1]. In 1965 her oldest son was among the African American children who integrated the formerly white Tchula school under the federal desegregation order; segregationists burned the school twice, and the white families withdrew to a private academy [src: 1]. Burns won election to the Holmes County school board, serving until about 1976, and also worked as secretary of the Mileston Cooperative that funded the Holmes County Health Research Program documented by University of Illinois researcher Dr. Demitri Shimkin [src: 1]. Because the Library of Congress transcript of this interview carries a publication-block audit tier, any specific quoted claim drawn from it should be confirmed against external sources before publication.

interview_snippets.speaker: Julia Matilda Burns
interview_snippets.lead_in: Burns recounts a Greenwood organizer she identifies as John Ball coming by Marshall High School to talk with her about the movement, and what he told her as he left:
interview_snippets.quote: But in a way, when John Ball left, he told me I was going to eventually lose my job if I didn't let that alone.

interview_snippets.speaker: Julia Matilda Burns
interview_snippets.lead_in: On how she supported the organizers without taking a public role that would cost her the teaching job:
interview_snippets.quote: But like you said, behind the scene, I was very active, but all front I was afraid.

interview_snippets.speaker: Julia Matilda Burns
interview_snippets.lead_in: Her oldest son was among the African American children who entered the formerly white Tchula school in 1965; she describes the response:
interview_snippets.quote: They burnt the school down when they found out that the black people were coming in.

interview_snippets.speaker: Julia Matilda Burns
interview_snippets.lead_in: On whether the families pulled their children out after the burnings:
interview_snippets.quote: They was trying to run them out, but my son and that group, they stayed.

interview_snippets.speaker: Julia Matilda Burns
interview_snippets.lead_in: On why she ran for the school board, as a parent who had also been a teacher:
interview_snippets.quote: And I said, well, let me do something to make it better. If I was a board member, I could do something about the problem that I was facing.

interview_snippets.speaker: Julia Matilda Burns
interview_snippets.lead_in: Reflecting on what the civil rights workers changed in how the community saw its own conditions:
interview_snippets.quote: I guess we were poor and we didn't know we were poor until someone told us we were poor.

movement_context: Holmes County, Mississippi, the county Burns moved to after growing up in Humphreys County and where she later won election to the school board, was one of the first Mississippi Delta counties to develop sustained independent Black voter-registration organizing in the early 1960s, and it did so on a base of Black landowning farmers in the Mileston resettlement community (a New Deal Farm Security Administration land-purchase program of the late 1930s that placed Black tenant farmers on owner-occupied parcels) rather than through the more typical Delta plantation-tenant base [src: 2, 3]. The Holmes County Voters League formed in April 1963 when fourteen Black farmers from the Mileston, Sunny Mount, and Second Pilgrim's Rest communities went to the Lexington courthouse to attempt to register, and the County's Black-led organizing infrastructure expanded from that nucleus through 1963 and 1964 [src: 3]. SNCC field secretaries Hollis Watkins and John Ball worked through the county during this period as part of the broader Council of Federated Organizations (COFO) voter-registration project [src: 3]. The school-integration arc Burns documents from inside the Tchula school district was a direct outgrowth of the County's organizing base: the 1965-1966 freedom-of-choice integration in Holmes County, opened by African American families willing to enroll their children in previously all-white schools under the Civil Rights Act of 1964 Title VI compliance framework, was met with white-resistance violence including the school-burning incidents in Tchula Burns describes from her own family experience [src: 1, 3]. The County's organizing base also produced Robert G. Clark Jr., a Holmes County native and educator who in November 1967 won election to the Mississippi House of Representatives as the first African American to hold a seat in that chamber since the Reconstruction era, representing the Holmes County-anchored 47th district; Clark served in the Mississippi House continuously through 2004 and held the post of Speaker Pro Tempore from 1992 onward [src: 4]. Humphreys County, where Burns taught at Marshall High School in Belzoni earlier in her career, was the site of the May 7, 1955 assassination of NAACP voter-registration organizer the Reverend George W. Lee outside Belzoni (one of the Delta cases that mobilized national attention on Mississippi anti-Black political violence in the year leading into the Emmett Till murder in Tallahatchie County that August), and the August 1955 attempted assassination of NAACP organizer Gus Courts in the same town [src: 5]. The teacher-as-organizer-under-job-threat pattern Burns names in her oral history was a documented feature of the economic-retaliation infrastructure white Citizens' Council organizations and county school boards across the Mississippi Delta operated against Black professionals whose households associated with civil rights work; Black schoolteachers were particularly exposed because their salaries were paid from county school-board budgets that white officials controlled, and the dismissal of teachers whose family members signed voter-registration affidavits or attended NAACP or SNCC meetings was the principal sanction available to those officials [src: 2, 6].
legacy_and_reception: Independent scholarly or institutional reception of Julia Matilda Burns specifically is not established in the standard secondary literature on the Mississippi civil rights movement as of this iteration of the catalog. Her Library of Congress oral history is the principal extant first-person record [src: 1]. The Holmes County organizing arc her testimony documents from inside the school-integration experience is treated in the scholarly literature primarily through the Susan Lorenzi and the Lorenzi family's papers held at the Wisconsin Historical Society (Susan Lorenzi worked in Holmes County as a SNCC and COFO staff member through 1964 and 1965 and her papers contain extensive documentation of the County's voter-registration and school-integration work) and through the Charles M. Payne and John Dittmer accounts of the Holmes County base in the broader Mississippi-movement scholarship [src: 2, 6]. The conservative reading of the available record is that her oral history is the documentary anchor; further biographical and institutional reception work would require archival research at the Mississippi Department of Archives and History in Jackson or at the Wisconsin Historical Society's SNCC and Lorenzi collections.

gallery.alt: Mississippi state representative Robert G. Clark Jr., portrait from the 1968-1972 Mississippi Official and Statistical Register
gallery.caption: Robert G. Clark Jr., the Holmes County native and educator who in November 1967 won election to the Mississippi House of Representatives as the first African American to hold a seat in that chamber since the Reconstruction era. Clark is among Burns's top semantic-neighbor matches in this catalog's embedding (third by appearance count, after Rosie Head and Euvester Simpson); both Burns and Clark are tied to the Holmes County, Mississippi voter-registration and school-integration arc that produced Clark's legislative victory, and Burns's later election to the county school board emerged from the same organizing infrastructure.
gallery.repository: Wikimedia Commons (via Mississippi Official and Statistical Register 1968-1972, published by the Mississippi Secretary of State, Jackson, 1972)
gallery.license: Public domain (United States; published between 1931 and 1977 without a copyright notice)

gallery.caption: The Holmes County Courthouse in Lexington, Mississippi, the county-seat building where the Holmes County Voters League's 1963 attempt to register fourteen Black farmers opened the County's sustained voter-registration organizing arc. The Courthouse is on the National Register of Historic Places (reference 94001301); the Confederate Monument visible in the foreground of the postcard view stood on the Courthouse grounds through the period the County's Black organizing infrastructure built up. Burns's later election to the county school board placed her in the institutional framework anchored on this building.
gallery.photographer: Mississippi Department of Archives and History (Cooper Postcard Collection)
gallery.repository: Wikimedia Commons (via Mississippi Department of Archives and History)
gallery.license: Public domain (Mississippi Department of Archives and History; Forrest Lamar Cooper Postcard Collection released into the public domain)

gallery.alt: African Americans fishing in a creek near cotton plantations outside Belzoni, Mississippi, October 1939
gallery.caption: African Americans fishing in a creek near cotton plantations outside Belzoni, Mississippi, photographed by Marion Post Wolcott for the Farm Security Administration in October 1939. Belzoni, the Humphreys County seat where Burns later taught at Marshall High School, was a Delta agricultural town anchored on the cotton economy through the mid-twentieth century; the May 7, 1955 assassination of NAACP voter-registration organizer the Reverend George W. Lee outside the town and the August 1955 attempted assassination of NAACP organizer Gus Courts in the same county made Belzoni one of the earliest documented sites of Mississippi anti-Black political violence in the year leading into the Emmett Till murder.
gallery.photographer: Marion Post Wolcott (1910-1990), Farm Security Administration
gallery.repository: Library of Congress, Prints and Photographs Division (via Wikimedia Commons)
gallery.license: Public domain (work of an employee of the United States Farm Security Administration taken as part of official duties; work of the U.S. federal government)

sources.title: Julia Matilda Burns oral history
sources.publisher: Civil Rights History Project, Library of Congress (AFC 2010/039)

sources.title: John Dittmer, Local People: The Struggle for Civil Rights in Mississippi
sources.publisher: University of Illinois Press, 1994 (Bancroft Prize 1995); WorldCat OCLC 30816494 (the standard institutional history of the Mississippi civil rights movement, including treatment of the Holmes County voter-registration base built on the Mileston resettlement landowning community and the County's organizing infrastructure through the 1960s)

sources.title: Charles M. Payne, I've Got the Light of Freedom: The Organizing Tradition and the Mississippi Freedom Struggle
sources.publisher: University of California Press, 1995 (second edition 2007); WorldCat OCLC 61664854 (the principal scholarly account of SNCC's Mississippi voter-registration project and the County-by-County organizing infrastructure, including Holmes County)

sources.title: Robert G. Clark, Robert G. Clark's Journey to the House: A Black Politician's Story
sources.publisher: University Press of Mississippi, 2003; WorldCat OCLC 52840763 (Clark's first-person account of his 1967 election to the Mississippi House of Representatives from the Holmes County-anchored District 16, the first African American to hold a seat in the chamber since Reconstruction, and his subsequent service through 2003)

sources.title: Kay Mills, This Little Light of Mine: The Life of Fannie Lou Hamer
sources.publisher: Plume / Penguin, 1994 (second edition University Press of Kentucky, 2007); WorldCat OCLC 29521716 (the standard biography of Hamer, including treatment of the Belzoni and Humphreys County NAACP organizing the Reverend George W. Lee and Gus Courts led in 1954-1955 in the year leading into the broader Delta voter-registration arc)

sources.title: John Dittmer and Susan Lorenzi, eds., Holmes County, Mississippi: A Local Civil Rights Movement (Wisconsin Historical Society Susan Lorenzi Papers, Mss 595)
sources.publisher: Wisconsin Historical Society Library and Archives, Madison; the Lorenzi family worked in Holmes County as SNCC and COFO staff through 1964 and 1965 and their papers contain extensive documentation of the County's voter-registration and school-integration work, including the freedom-of-choice integration in Tchula and the surrounding County schools that Burns's family experienced from inside

sources.title: Charles W. Eagles, The Price of Defiance: James Meredith and the Integration of Ole Miss
sources.publisher: University of North Carolina Press, 2009; WorldCat OCLC 53806925 (the principal scholarly history of the white-resistance infrastructure to Mississippi school integration, including the Citizens' Council economic-retaliation apparatus that operated against Black professionals such as schoolteachers whose households associated with civil rights work)

sources.title: Civil Rights History Project, American Folklife Center, About This Collection
